mcqs.question: Two processes open the same file and both perform writes. If the OS uses UNIX semantics, what happens when Process A writes 'Hello' and Process B writes 'World'?

options.text: Both writes are serialized; file contains either 'HelloWorld' or 'WorldHello' based on timing
Only the last write persists in the file
An error is raised because both processes have exclusive access
The file contains both strings in an undefined order

mcqs.explanation: Under UNIX semantics, writes are sequentially consistent. The order of writes depends on timing, but the file will contain one string before the other (either 'HelloWorld' or 'WorldHello'), not interleaved characters.

mcqs.question: A process acquires a shared (read) lock on a file. Can another process acquire an exclusive (write) lock simultaneously?

options.text: Yes, always possible
No, exclusive lock cannot be granted while shared locks exist
Only if the OS uses advisory locking
Only if both processes belong to the same user

mcqs.explanation: In most locking mechanisms, exclusive locks cannot be granted while any shared lock exists because this would violate data consistency - a writer needs to see all previous writes without any concurrent readers potentially reading partial/inconsistent data.

mcqs.question: In the readers-writers problem with priority given to writers, if a writer is waiting, what happens to new reader requests?

options.text: New readers are granted immediate access
New readers must wait until the waiting writer completes
An error is returned to readers
Readers are granted but writer starvation is prevented

mcqs.explanation: In writer-priority readers-writers solution, to prevent writer starvation, new reader requests are blocked if a writer is waiting. This ensures writers don't wait indefinitely while new readers keep arriving.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is an example of advisory locking in UNIX?

options.text: flock() system call
fcntl() with F_SETLKW
lockf() with mandatory locking enabled
All of the above use mandatory locking

mcqs.explanation: flock() is purely advisory - processes must voluntarily use it. fcntl() can be used for both advisory and mandatory locking depending on system configuration. lockf() is also advisory by default but can enforce mandatory locks.

mcqs.question: Process P1 holds Lock A and waits for Lock B, while Process P2 holds Lock B and waits for Lock A. This is an example of:

options.text: Deadlock
Starvation
Race condition
Priority inversion

mcqs.explanation: This is a classic deadlock scenario where two processes are each waiting for a lock held by the other, creating a circular wait. Neither can proceed without the other releasing its lock.

mcqs.question: In copy-on-write (COW) file sharing does the actual, when copying of data occur?

options.text: When any process opens the file
When a process attempts to modify the shared data
Only when the file is closed
Immediately after the fork() system call

mcqs.explanation: Copy-on-write defers the actual copying until a process attempts to modify the shared data. Initially, all processes share the same memory pages. When a process writes, a private copy is created for that process.

mcqs.question: A file server grants a 30-second lease to a client. What happens after 30 seconds?

options.text: The lease is automatically renewed
The client must release the file immediately
The lease expires and other clients can request access
The file is deleted from the server

mcqs.explanation: A lease is a time-limited lock. After expiration, the server can grant the file to another client. The original client may need to re-request and may get denied if another client has the file.

mcqs.question: Under session semantics (used by some distributed systems), when are file changes visible to other users?

options.text: Immediately to all users
Only after the file is closed
Only after the user logs out
Changes are never visible to other users

mcqs.explanation: Session semantics makes changes visible only within a user's session. Other users see the file's state at the time they opened it, and only see changes after they close and reopen the file.

mcqs.question: What is the main advantage of file locking over explicit synchronization using semaphores for file access?

options.text: File locking is faster
File locking is managed by the OS and doesn't require programmer intervention
File locking prevents all race conditions
File locking uses less memory

mcqs.explanation: File locking is OS-managed, providing a standardized way to coordinate access without requiring programmers to implement custom synchronization primitives. However, it doesn't prevent all race conditions (especially with advisory locking).

mcqs.question: Three processes open the same file for reading. How many read locks can be held simultaneously?

options.text: Only one read lock is allowed
Three read locks can be held simultaneously (shared access)
Zero - reads don't require locks in UNIX
Depends on the file size

mcqs.explanation: Multiple processes can hold shared (read) locks simultaneously because reading doesn't modify data. This is the essence of the readers-writers problem - allowing concurrent reads while restricting writes.

mcqs.question: Which technique helps prevent writer starvation in file sharing?

options.text: Giving priority to readers over writers
Allowing multiple writers simultaneously
Queueing writers and ensuring no new readers start when writers are queued
Using only exclusive locks

mcqs.explanation: To prevent writer starvation, when a writer is waiting, new readers should be blocked from acquiring locks. This is part of the writer-priority solution to the readers-writers problem.

mcqs.question: What is the primary purpose of file locking in operating systems?

options.text: To improve file access speed
To prevent concurrent access to files
To coordinate access to shared files and prevent data inconsistency
To allocate file space efficiently

mcqs.explanation: The primary purpose of file locking is coordination - ensuring that when multiple processes access shared files, the data remains consistent. It prevents race conditions and corrupted data.

mcqs.question: A process attempts to acquire a lock using non-blocking fcntl() (F_SETLK). What happens if the lock is unavailable?

options.text: The process waits indefinitely for the lock
The process returns immediately with an error
The process is terminated by the OS
The lock is granted after a timeout

mcqs.explanation: F_SETLK attempts to acquire the lock without waiting. If unavailable, it returns immediately with an error (EACCES or EAGAIN). F_SETLKW (with 'W' for wait) would block until the lock becomes available.

mcqs.question: In mandatory locking, what happens if a process tries to access a locked file region without checking the lock?

options.text: The OS allows access and data may be corrupted
The OS blocks the access at the kernel level
The system issues a warning but allows access
The process is killed by the OS

mcqs.explanation: In mandatory locking, the OS enforces locks at the kernel level. Even if a process doesn't check for locks, any attempt to access a locked file region will be blocked by the kernel, ensuring data integrity.

mcqs.question: What problem can occur with lock upgrade in file systems?

options.text: Lock upgrade is always successful
Lock upgrade can cause deadlock if not handled properly
Lock upgrade requires root privileges
Lock upgrade is not supported in modern OS

mcqs.explanation: Converting a shared lock to an exclusive lock (lock upgrade) can cause deadlock if another process is also trying to upgrade. For example, if two processes both hold shared locks and both try to upgrade simultaneously, they may each wait for the other's shared lock to be released, creating deadlock.
